([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Stadtkirche Biel*) is a [Swiss Reformed](/wiki/Swiss_Reformed_Church "Swiss Reformed Church") church in [Biel/Bienne](/wiki/Biel/Bienne "Biel/Bienne"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). Built in the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") style, its construction started around 1451 and was mostly complete in 1470. The church is considered one of the most significant late-Gothic churches in Switzerland[[1]](#cite_note-GSK-1) and is a Swiss [Cultural Property of National Significance](/wiki/Swiss_Inventory_of_Cultural_Property_of_National_and_Regional_Significance "Swiss Inventory of Cultural Property of National and Regional Significance").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The **City Church of Biel** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Stadtkirche Biel*) is a [Swiss Reformed](/wiki/Swiss_Reformed_Church "Swiss Reformed Church") church in [Biel/Bienne](/wiki/Biel/Bienne "Biel/Bienne"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). Built in the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") style, its construction started around 1451 and was mostly complete in 1470. The church is considered one of the most significant late-Gothic churches in Switzerland[[1]](#cite_note-GSK-1) and is a Swiss [Cultural Property of National Significance](/wiki/Swiss_Inventory_of_Cultural_Property_of_National_and_Regional_Significance "Swiss Inventory of Cultural Property of National and Regional Significance").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Church_of_Biel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The current church was built on the site of two earlier churches, a [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") building and an early Gothic building. Very little is known of the previous churches, though the old [sacristy](/wiki/Sacristy "Sacristy") now forms a [crypt](/wiki/Crypt "Crypt") below the [choir](/wiki/Choir_(architecture) "Choir (architecture)").[[3]](#cite_note-alt-3) The town of Biel first appears in records between 1225 and 1230 while one of the earlier churches is first mentioned in 1228. This and the current church were dedicated to [St. Benedict](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1367, much of the town, including the church, was destroyed in a fire. Construction began on the current church in 1451 under the leadership of the master builder Wenzlin of Bohemia, who remained on the site until his death in 1465. Primary construction finished up about 5 years later in 1470. The tower of the previous Gothic church had to be repaired and strengthened and was incorporated into the new building. In 1480 additional height was added to bring the bells above the new roof. However, while finishing construction a wall collapsed. The top of the tower, the bells and an unlucky worker all fell into the street below. Master builder Henmann Ulfinger spent about 10 years rebuilding the tower, finishing in 1490. In 1549 Hans Dyck replaced the old bell tower roof with a steeply pointed spire and four small spires, a design which remains today. The five current bells were added in the 19th and 20th centuries; three in 1882, one in 1947 and one in 1955.[[3]](#cite_note-alt-3) The church was partly rebuilt in the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") in 1781-83. The Baroque elements were replaced with [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") elements in 1864-84 under the direction of Hans Rycher. The Gothic Revival elements were removed and the original Gothic design and decoration were repaired in 1908-11 by Emanuel Jirka Propper.[[1]](#cite_note-GSK-1)

## The building exterior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Church_of_Biel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The building exterior")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Floor_Plan_Biel_Stadtkirche.png/250px-Floor_Plan_Biel_Stadtkirche.png)](/wiki/File:Floor_Plan_Biel_Stadtkirche.png)

Floor plan of the church

The church is an uneven, mostly rectangular, sharply [beveled](/wiki/Bevel "Bevel") on the north-west corner [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica"). It lacks a [crossing](/wiki/Crossing_(architecture) "Crossing (architecture)") and [transepts](/wiki/Transept "Transept"). The polygonal choir and [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") share a single roof and there are side chapels between the [buttresses](/wiki/Buttress "Buttress") on the south side. The massive tower makes up the north-east corner. The western portal is partly interrupted by the beveled north-west corner and so has an asymmetric arraignment of a pointed arch [portal](/wiki/Portal_(architecture) "Portal (architecture)") and windows.[[1]](#cite_note-GSK-1)

## The interior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Church_of_Biel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The interior")]

The church has a central nave with two uneven [side aisles](/wiki/Aisle#Church_architecture "Aisle") and four side chapels. The nave is divided into four [bays](/wiki/Bay_(architecture) "Bay (architecture)"), supported by large octagonal [capital-less](/wiki/Capital_(architecture) "Capital (architecture)") [pillars](/wiki/Pillar "Pillar"). The roof in the eastern (and older) part of the church is supported by [rib vaults](/wiki/Rib_vault "Rib vault") while the western part has [lierne](/wiki/Lierne_(vault) "Lierne (vault)") [vaults](/wiki/Vault_(architecture) "Vault (architecture)"). The wall behind the choir is pierced with three high pointed arch windows. The windows are filled with 15th century [stained glass](/wiki/Stained_glass "Stained glass") which depict events from the life of St. Benedict. The nave and choir together are 36 meters (118 ft) long, 7.5 m (25 ft) wide and 14 m (46 ft) high.[[3]](#cite_note-alt-3)

## Organs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Church_of_Biel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Organs")]

### Main organ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Church_of_Biel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Main organ")]

This [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") organ was built by Jacques Besancon above the western portal. It was first played on 20 September 1783 and was damaged in the 25 July 1855 earthquake. Twenty years later, in 1875, it was finally repaired and put back in service. It was repaired, cleaned and partly rebuilt in 1902 and again in 1943. At that time, the organ had more than 55 registers, distributed over three manuals and pedals. In 2011 it was repaired by the Metzler Orgel company and put back into service.[[5]](#cite_note-parish-5) After the 2011 work, the organ has a total of 51 registers, including an extended register and a transmission, on four manuals and a pedal. A special feature is the 4th manual, called the *Winddynamisches Werk*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Swallow's nest organ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Church_of_Biel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Swallow's nest organ")]

A [Swallow's nest organ](/wiki/Swallow%27s_nest_organ "Swallow's nest organ"), built in 1994 by Metzler Orgelbau (Dietikon), is located on the north wall of the church. It replaces the 1517 swallow's nest organ which was built by Hans Tugi and was destroyed in rioting during the Reformation in 1527.[[3]](#cite_note-alt-3) Today's instrument has nine [registers](/wiki/Registration_(organ) "Registration (organ)"), Rückpositiv (FGAB-g2a 2: Gedackt 8', Principal 4') and Manual (CDEFGAB-g2a2: Praestant 8', Coppel 8', Octave 4', Waldflöte 2', Mixtur IV, Sesquialtera II, Regal 8'). The pedal (CDEFGAB-c1) is attached to the manual. The instrument is equipped with a [Tremulant](/wiki/Tremulant "Tremulant") as well as the effect registers Vogelgesang, Zimbelglöcklein as well as with a Kalkantenglocke. It is medium-toned and is designed especially for the performance of organ music of Late Gothic, the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") and the [Early Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque"). The painted wing doors were completed in 1995.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
